title: Super Tuesday

jokes.text: Mayor Splett, how does it feel to be the hero of 7-Eleven? / Oh, I'm no hero. I'm just a mayor.
jokes.setup: Reporter frames Richard in heroic, grandiose terms
jokes.punchline: Richard deflects with the most bare-minimum, literal self-description possible — 'I'm just a mayor'
jokes.explanation: Classic Richard deadpan — the humor comes entirely from his complete lack of ego or self-awareness. 'I'm just a mayor' is perfectly calibrated Splett: not humble-bragging, just literally stating his job title as if it cancels out heroism. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Poll watchers are reporting that over 48,000 black voters were turned away. / I think the Chinese just delivered.
jokes.setup: A staffer reports a massive, serious voter suppression event affecting Black voters
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately reframes it as a delivery from her Chinese backers — treating voter suppression as a campaign service
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The juxtaposition is brutal: a civil rights atrocity treated as a Seamless order. The economy is perfect — no extra words. It's deeply Selina-specific: her casual transactional amorality, her total indifference to anyone not named Selina Meyer. The 'delivered' wordplay (food delivery / political delivery) adds a layer. This is Veep at its darkest and best.

jokes.text: 'If I need another Washington douche, I'll go to the M Street Rite-Aid.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks how they were fired
jokes.punchline: Selina's firing is delivered as a memorably contemptuous quip equating Washington staffers with drugstore feminine hygiene products
jokes.explanation: This is a standout Veep insult — specific (M Street Rite-Aid is a real D.C. location, adding geographic texture), vulgar but clever (the douche metaphor for Washington insiders), and delivered as a secondhand quote which makes it land even harder. The specificity of 'M Street' elevates it above a generic insult. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: That's an hour and 29 minutes too long in that former Indian concentration camp.
jokes.setup: Staffer announces an hour-and-a-half rally in Oklahoma City
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses Oklahoma City with a historically loaded, casually delivered racial slur-adjacent description
jokes.explanation: Selina's casual historical insensitivity is a core character beat. The math joke ('29 minutes too long') adds structure. The Oklahoma/Native American history reference is pointed without being gratuitous — it's satirizing Selina's ignorant entitlement rather than Native Americans.

jokes.text: Here's your speech, ma'am. I dumbed it down even more. / Good, 'cause the last one looked like the toilet bowl after William F. Buckley ate a thesaurus.
jokes.setup: Leon presents a dumbed-down speech
jokes.punchline: Selina's response comparing the previous speech to a thesaurus-eating Buckley's toilet bowl
jokes.explanation: Outstanding insult construction. 'William F. Buckley ate a thesaurus' is a brilliant specific-to-the-joke cultural reference — Buckley's famously verbose vocabulary weaponized as a gross-out image. The toilet metaphor is perfectly calibrated vulgarity. Could only be Selina: she's insulting her own campaign's intellectualism while demanding it be stupider. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Kemi has been getting some death threats. / Well, then we should leak some of my death threats, OK? I get some, don't I?
jokes.setup: Kemi's death threats are raised as a political concern (she's getting Secret Service protection)
jokes.punchline: Selina's competitive jealousy extends to wanting to publicize her own death threats as a political asset
jokes.explanation: Peak Selina character comedy. The competitive narcissism of wanting to 'leak' death threats as a PR strategy — and the anxious '...I get some, don't I?' — is both funny and character-perfect. The 'I get some, don't I?' tag adds a beat of real pathos/comedy: she's actually worried she's not important enough to be threatened. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: 'I'm gonna break your fat arms and strangle you--' / Wait. I don't have fat arms. / That's just criminally insane-- and regular insane.
jokes.setup: Leon reads a death threat aloud as a potential PR asset
jokes.punchline: Selina's objection to the threat is that her arms aren't fat — she ignores the violence entirely
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection — the expected response to a death threat is fear; Selina's is vanity. 'That's just criminally insane — and regular insane' as a parenthetical is a nice button. Character-specific: Selina's solipsism is consistent. The escalation of 'Pick another one. A plausible one' continues the joke well.

jokes.text: Pick another one. A plausible one. / 'Someone should put a bullet in your shriveled old face.' / No!
jokes.setup: Having rejected the first death threat for being physically inaccurate, Selina asks for a plausible one
jokes.punchline: The 'plausible' threat insults her age and appearance in a way that hits harder
jokes.explanation: Strong escalation from joke 7. The logic that a death threat calling her old is more personally offensive than one threatening her life is perfect Selina. 'Shriveled old face' lands as the real insult. Good escalation structure.

jokes.text: Just make up some death threats that are nicer. / OK, I'll come up with a dozen.
jokes.setup: Real death threats are either inaccurate or offensive to Selina's vanity
jokes.punchline: The solution: fabricate flattering death threats; Leon commits to 'a dozen' as casually as if ordered to write thank-you notes
jokes.explanation: The absurdist cap on the running gag. 'A dozen' is the perfect quantity — bureaucratic, precise, and completely insane. Leon's unfazed professional compliance is the joke. Good economy.

jokes.text: Gary, go find out where we are.
jokes.setup: Selina asks where they are before going onstage
jokes.punchline: She doesn't know what state she's in and sends her bag-man to find out, as if geographic orientation is Gary's job
jokes.explanation: Efficient beat of political satire — the candidate is so disconnected from reality she doesn't know what state she's in during a Super Tuesday swing. Gary's assigned to find out. Short, sharp, characterful.

jokes.text: We no longer use the Rosa story. / What are you talking about?! It kills every time. Tears! / Rosa was deported this morning. / Well, that's impossible, because I made her up. / Well, no, actually, ma'am, there's a Rosa Sanchez in Tennessee who has four kids and the exact same dead-end jobs.
jokes.setup: Leon tells Selina she can't use her go-to tearjerker story about Rosa
jokes.punchline: The twist: Selina invented Rosa, but accidentally described a real person who was then deported based on details in her speech
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The construction is extraordinary: Selina invented a fictional immigrant character for cheap emotional manipulation; that character turned out to be real; and Selina's speech caused that real person to be deported. It's a perfect Veep escalation — each beat makes things worse and more absurd while remaining causally coherent. Selina's outrage that 'I made her up' before being told Rosa is real is perfectly timed. Dark and brilliant.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, right out of my Stephen Glass-hole.
jokes.setup: Realizing Selina's fictional speech character is a real person who got deported
jokes.punchline: Leon coins 'Stephen Glass-hole' — a portmanteau of Stephen Glass (the fabricating journalist) and 'glass-hole' — applied to himself as the speechwriter who created the fictional character
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay. 'Stephen Glass-hole' is a genuinely brilliant coinage: it references the notorious journalism fabricator Stephen Glass, and blends it with 'asshole' to create a self-deprecating term. The joke requires the audience to know who Stephen Glass is, which is exactly the right register for Veep's audience. Extremely quotable and original — this is word-construction comedy at a very high level.

jokes.text: Tell my attorney to meet me in the next filler state we're in.
jokes.setup: Selina learns Andrew is cutting a deal and needs her lawyer
jokes.punchline: She refers to whatever state she's flying to as a 'filler state' — dismissing entire U.S. states as logistical inconveniences
jokes.explanation: Efficient satirical characterization — Selina treats swing states as infrastructure rather than places with people. 'Filler state' is a small but good coinage.

jokes.text: 'Cause last I heard, you can't run for office and be in prison. / Ben: Maybe in the House you can.
jokes.setup: Selina says you can't run for office from prison
jokes.punchline: Ben notes that in the House of Representatives, you probably can — a dim view of Congress's standards
jokes.explanation: Clean quick joke. Ben's deadpan institutional cynicism is well-deployed. The joke works because it's actually true — Eugene Debs ran for president from prison. Short and effective.

jokes.text: Your unfavorables are rising past 'accidental ethnic slur' right into 'men's room incident.'
jokes.setup: Ben reports Selina's poll numbers are deteriorating
jokes.punchline: The sliding scale of political scandals — 'accidental ethnic slur' to 'men's room incident' — as a measurement unit for unfavorability ratings
jokes.explanation: Brilliant. The conceit of using political scandals as measurement units is deeply original. The specific two examples ('accidental ethnic slur' vs 'men's room incident') are perfectly calibrated — the men's room incident evokes a specific type of political disgrace (Larry Craig) without naming it, which is funnier. Could only exist in a political satire. Highly quotable, likely a fan favorite line.

jokes.text: This is the face of clinical depression, ma'am. / With the hair of a mental patient. / Oh, my kingdom for a beret.
jokes.setup: Selina encounters Catherine, who looks visibly depressed
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'sympathy' for her clinically depressed daughter consists entirely of critiquing her appearance, followed by wishing for a beret to cover it
jokes.explanation: The 'kingdom for a beret' is a nice Shakespearean touch on a shallow maternal instinct. Selina diagnosing her daughter's appearance rather than her mental health is consistent and dark. The exchange flows well as a three-beat unit.

jokes.text: if anyone should be depressed, it's me, but I am stable as fuck. / Yeah, you are!
jokes.setup: Selina is dismissing Catherine's depression by pointing to her own circumstances
jokes.punchline: Gary's enthusiastic affirmation of Selina's 'stability' is absurd given everything that's happening; his sycophancy is the joke
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'Yeah, you are!' is the joke — his reflexive validation of everything Selina says, even a patently false claim about her stability during a crisis, is pure character comedy. The irony is doing heavy lifting.

jokes.text: Andrew might be cutting a deal, which would just destroy me. / I have to stop you there, ma'am. Andrew has hired me as his new attorney. / Oh -- Thank God. / So you can talk him out of this deal, right? / But it's a very good deal. For him.
jokes.setup: Selina is relieved to see Karen — her lawyer — and assumes Karen will help her
jokes.punchline: Karen is now Andrew's attorney and the deal is very good — for Andrew
jokes.explanation: Clean misdirection — the relief of 'Oh, thank God' and then 'But it's a very good deal. For him' is a perfectly structured reversal. Karen's lawyerly precision ('For him') is the punchline and it lands well.

jokes.text: Karen... you understand that... when I am elected president again, I can issue Andrew a full pardon. Right? / I wish you hadn't told me that. / And yet I'm also glad that you did. / I might be able to use it against you. / I wouldn't... unless I have to.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to leverage a pardon promise with Karen
jokes.punchline: Karen's response cycles through 'wish you hadn't told me / glad you did / might use it against you / wouldn't unless I have to' — a portrait of studied ethical ambiguity that reveals Karen as hilariously, perfectly useless as an advisor
jokes.explanation: Karen's running gag from earlier seasons — she refuses to commit to any position — is perfectly deployed here. Each clause of her response reverses the previous one: every statement is immediately qualified into meaninglessness. It's a brilliantly structured character joke. 'I wouldn't... unless I have to' is the perfect closer.

jokes.text: Well, this has been a dry fuck on a sandy beach.
jokes.setup: The meeting with Karen has produced nothing useful for Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's summary: 'a dry fuck on a sandy beach' — an image that conveys painful, gritty friction with no pleasure
jokes.explanation: A Veep-signature vulgar metaphor that's genuinely fresh. 'Dry fuck on a sandy beach' is vivid, original, perfectly economical, and characteristically Selina. It's a great exit line from a frustrating scene. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Any luck with the human Mobius strip? / Andrew's cutting a deal. / I could go to jail. / I don't look good in jumpsuits. / I mean, I do, but I don't. Ya know.
jokes.setup: Ben asks about the meeting with Karen (the human Möbius strip — someone who goes nowhere and comes back to the same point)
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to potential imprisonment is a wardrobe concern, then a double-reversal on the wardrobe concern
jokes.explanation: 'Human Möbius strip' for Karen is a wonderful descriptor — it captures her circular non-advice perfectly. Selina's jumpsuit vanity bit is a well-executed character joke. 'I mean, I do, but I don't. Ya know.' is a nice self-correcting Selina tic.

jokes.text: call it the Washington Post like a non-asshole
jokes.setup: A staffer says 'WaPo'
jokes.punchline: Selina corrects the shorthand as a marker of douchiness
jokes.explanation: Quick, sharp observational bit that feels authentic to media/political culture. The joke is in Selina's specific contempt for insider shorthand — ironic given her own insider status. Good economy.

jokes.text: stop staring at me like I'm some sort of teenage runaway that you just strangled.
jokes.setup: A staffer is giving Selina a significant look after delivering bad news
jokes.punchline: Selina's description of the look is shockingly violent and specific — she tells someone to stop looking at her like they've just killed her
jokes.explanation: Strong Veep-register dark comedy — the image is vivid and surprising. The specificity of 'teenage runaway' adds character to the metaphor. Slightly undercut by being one of many insults in a rapid-fire scene.

jokes.text: this is like having two different Senators with their hands up my skirt. / Which actually happened to me once during a Supreme Court confirmation.
jokes.setup: Selina is complaining about being squeezed by two simultaneous crises
jokes.punchline: She uses a sexual harassment metaphor, then casually reveals it literally happened to her at a Supreme Court confirmation — treating a workplace assault as a throwaway anecdote
jokes.explanation: Two-stage joke: the metaphor is vivid and political, then the reveal that it literally happened — and during a Supreme Court confirmation — is a perfect dark escalation. Selina's casual dismissal of the incident as an aside is both character comedy and sharp political satire about normalizing harassment in institutions. Excellent.

jokes.text: The Joseph Goebbels playbook. Accuse the other side of that which you are guilty. / Steal from the best.
jokes.setup: The team discusses how to handle the foreign interference story
jokes.punchline: Selina endorses the Goebbels strategy without hesitation; Ben adds 'steal from the best' as casual endorsement
jokes.explanation: The Goebbels citation is blunt satire — effective because it's 2018/2019 Veep and the reference lands with real cultural weight. Ben's 'steal from the best' is a good button. Slightly on-the-nose for Veep's usual subtlety but works in context.

jokes.text: Just give it to one of those gay-converting Baptist colleges to fund a statue of a gold-plated Jesus fucking a triceratops.
jokes.setup: Gary needs to spend Chinese dark money on faith-based causes ASAP
jokes.punchline: Selina's idea of plausible faith-based expenditure: a statue of Jesus having sex with a dinosaur at a conversion therapy college
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific absurdism. Each element is satirically loaded: gay-converting Baptist college (anti-LGBTQ institutions), gold-plated Jesus (prosperity gospel excess), triceratops (creationism/dinosaurs/religious lunacy). The combination is deeply funny. 'Wow.' from the staffer is the perfect understated button. Very quotable.

jokes.text: All right, Congressman Slender Man. / Don't say his name.
jokes.setup: Amy addresses Jonah by his Secret Service/campaign nickname
jokes.punchline: The running gag of Jonah being called 'Slender Man' — and his objection to it — is established
jokes.explanation: Running gag setup. The 'Slender Man' nickname fits Jonah (tall, angular, creepy) and his reaction 'Don't say his name' — as if invoking it summons something — adds a layer of absurdity. Good character-specific comedy.

jokes.text: No, I told Beth that we could go to Arkansas so she could give me a hand job in a hot spring. / It's my birthday.
jokes.setup: Amy announces they're staying in Florida for the primary
jokes.punchline: Jonah's objection: he promised his staffer/girlfriend a hot spring hand job for his birthday
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jonah character beat — the birthday hand job complaint is simultaneously pathetic, cringe-inducing, and oddly specific (hot spring). 'It's my birthday' from Beth as a tag is the right button. Deeply Jonah.

jokes.text: especially with melanoma-loving swamp fuckers, storm-ravaged climate deniers, and deadbeat dads... and deadbeat moms. / Those are my peeps.
jokes.setup: Amy describes Jonah's Florida base
jokes.punchline: Jonah enthusiastically claims the litany of Florida degradation stereotypes as his constituency — 'Those are my peeps'
jokes.explanation: 'Melanoma-loving swamp fuckers' and 'storm-ravaged climate deniers' are excellent Florida characterizations — specific, mean, earned by the satire. Jonah's gleeful ownership of his base is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Thirty-eight dollars? That's like a shitty gift that you get from your asshole grandmother. / If Selina Meyer did this, she'd get, like, a thousand dollars because she's, like, a thousand years old.
jokes.setup: The campaign announces a $38 birthday fundraiser for Jonah's 38th birthday
jokes.punchline: Jonah himself calls it a shitty gift, then pivots to attacking Selina's age
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Jonah has no filter. The 'thousand dollars / thousand years old' construction is decent. Not exceptional but works.

jokes.text: Holy shit, Bruckheimer, when you get an abortion, you're supposed to leave the mangled fetus at the clinic, not staple it to the skeleton of a gay condor and run it for president.
jokes.setup: Dan arrives at the Jonah Ryan campaign
jokes.punchline: Dan's greeting to the campaign is a grotesquely elaborate insult comparing the whole operation to a botched abortion stapled to a gay condor skeleton
jokes.explanation: An exceptional Veep insult — Dan's signature mode is baroque, elaborately specific invective, and this exemplifies it perfectly. 'Staple it to the skeleton of a gay condor and run it for president' is genuinely inspired — the specificity of 'gay condor skeleton' is what makes it transcendent. It's original, surprising at every clause, and could only come from Dan. One of the episode's top jokes.

jokes.text: Well, hello, darlin'. / What Saudi prince's rape dungeon did you finger-trowel your way out of?
jokes.setup: Amy arrives at Jonah's campaign
jokes.punchline: Jonah's greeting to Amy — the 'Saudi prince's rape dungeon' insult with 'finger-trowel' as the escape method
jokes.explanation: 'Finger-trowel' is a great specific detail — digging with fingernails. The 'Saudi prince's rape dungeon' is darkly topical. Good Veep insult register. Slightly less elegant than the best examples in this episode.

jokes.text: Uh... Jonah's rape dungeon? / Oh ho! In your face!
jokes.setup: Amy is asked where she came from (following Jonah's 'rape dungeon' insult)
jokes.punchline: Amy pivots the insult back to Jonah; his 'In your face!' response is an absurd failure to understand he's been insulted
jokes.explanation: The reversal works and Jonah's 'In your face!' — celebrating being called a rapist dungeon operator — is a perfect beat of his complete social obliviousness. Quick and effective.

jokes.text: All right, anyway, I have the pleasure of informing you, Congressman Slender Man-- / Mm-mm. / Amy: Nope, beat you to it.
jokes.setup: Amy attempts the Slender Man nickname again
jokes.punchline: Jonah tries to preempt it with 'Mm-mm'; Amy confirms she beat him to saying it
jokes.explanation: Running gag second beat — competent but not as strong as the setup. The comedic value is in Jonah's preemptive protest and Amy's smug confirmation. Functional.

jokes.text: Youngblood? Oh, that's my favorite Rob Lowe movie. / ... / Oh, yeah! This is like looking in a mirror. / Yeah, a hot mirror.
jokes.setup: The Secret Service agent is introduced as Agent Youngblood
jokes.punchline: Jonah's 'looking in a mirror' self-comparison to a conventionally handsome agent; the agent deadpans 'a hot mirror'
jokes.explanation: 'A hot mirror' from the agent is a nice deadpan beat — it agrees with the comparison while implicitly contrasting Jonah's appearance. The Rob Lowe reference (Youngblood is a Rob Lowe film) is a fun double-meaning on the agent's name. Solid.

jokes.text: Chances are you're still likely to get assassinated, but the killer, may God guide his hand, will just have to work a little harder.
jokes.setup: Amy explains what the Secret Service protection means for Jonah
jokes.punchline: 'May God guide his hand' — Amy actively wishing for Jonah's assassination while technically explaining his protection
jokes.explanation: Outstanding Amy line. The formality of 'may God guide his hand' — a phrase of blessing applied to a would-be assassin — is brilliantly structured. It's Amy at her most Amy: professionally managing Jonah while openly rooting for his death. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Sincerest congratulations on all your life choices, Amy. / Thank you. It pays quite well. / Have a good weepy slide down the shower wall this evening.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy have their first reunion on the episode
jokes.punchline: 'A weepy slide down the shower wall' — Dan's parting image for Amy's evening is a perfectly vivid portrait of existential despair
jokes.explanation: The 'weepy slide down the shower wall' is a great visual metaphor — it's specific enough to be genuinely funny (the slidedown being the visual joke) and it fits Dan's arsenal of targeted devastation. 'It pays quite well' from Amy is a good defensive rejoinder.

jokes.text: Will's got a full day ahead of him. Tell 'em what you gotta do, Will. / I was hoping to finally finish my passion project. / Which is? / Rerouting my urethra to behind my balls so that I have to sit to pee like a real girl.
jokes.setup: Amy asks Will to share his schedule
jokes.punchline: Will's passion project is elective urethral rerouting surgery to 'sit to pee like a real girl'
jokes.explanation: Deeply weird and original joke. Will's character has always been odd, and this takes him into genuinely unexpected territory. 'Like a real girl' as the stated aspiration is an odd gender/identity joke that's more surreal than offensive. The specific anatomical detail ('behind my balls') is what makes it absurdist rather than cheap.

jokes.text: Sit your hawk asses down! / Not you, Richard.
jokes.setup: Dan addresses the press in Richard's name, asserting control
jokes.punchline: He accidentally tells Richard to sit too, then immediately corrects himself
jokes.explanation: Quick beat of Dan's overreach corrected. 'Hawk asses' is a nice insult for reporters. The 'Not you, Richard' is a small character beat showing Dan's odd relationship with Richard. Functional but minor.

jokes.text: Except for the 7-Eleven truthers. They say that no Jewish people were working in the store that day, but there's no Jewish people in Lurlene, so...
jokes.setup: Dan says the entire country thinks Richard is a hero
jokes.punchline: There's a conspiracy theory contingent ('7-Eleven truthers') whose anti-Semitic complaint about the 7-Eleven story is undercut by the fact that there are no Jewish people in Lurlene anyway
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical joke about internet conspiracy theories and anti-Semitic tropes, with the self-defeating qualifier (no Jewish people in the town anyway). The '7-Eleven truthers' coinage is good. Dan delivers it as pure fact which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Dan, there's no easy way to say this, but given your reputation, there's no dipping your quill in the company ink. / Copy that. / I mean sex. / Got it. / By that, I mean Willa.
jokes.setup: Richard introduces his new employee Willa and needs to set ground rules with Dan
jokes.punchline: Richard's escalating clarifications — 'I mean sex,' 'By that, I mean Willa' — each more specific than necessary, delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Excellent Richard joke. The three-step clarification structure is perfect: 'quill in the company ink' → 'I mean sex' → 'By that, I mean Willa.' Each step strips away another layer of metaphor because Richard doesn't trust Dan to decode euphemism. It's entirely Richard — his earnest over-explanation is the comedy. Great structure.

jokes.text: Mike: I wouldn't be doing my job, Madam President, if I didn't ask the question that's on everybody's mind: / Is that a new hairstyle?
jokes.setup: Mike sets up what sounds like a tough question for the press interview
jokes.punchline: The question 'on everybody's mind' is about Selina's hair — Mike's softball journalism is as bad as ever
jokes.explanation: Strong Mike character joke — the elaborate preamble ('I wouldn't be doing my job...') sets up what sounds like a serious question, then the punchline is hair. The misdirection is clean and the character integration is excellent (Mike as a terrible journalist is a core running gag).

jokes.text: Well, what we really should be talking about is Senator Talbot's husband and the longstanding accusations against him. / Oh. Well, let's go with that one. / That sounds juicy.
jokes.setup: Selina deflects from Andrew's indictment by planting a story about a rival
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'That sounds juicy' — a journalist uncritically accepting planted opposition research because it's entertaining
jokes.explanation: Mike's 'That sounds juicy' perfectly encapsulates his journalistic inadequacy. He's being manipulated in real time and finds it delightful. Good character moment.

jokes.text: Well, you said it, not me, but I also say it.
jokes.setup: Mike suggests the press is biased against Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's response — 'you said it, not me, but I also say it' — is a beautiful piece of political doublespeak that simultaneously disclaims and adopts the position
jokes.explanation: A gem. The compression of political non-denial denial into nine words is exceptional. 'You said it, not me, but I also say it' captures the precise mechanism of plausible deniability while abandoning it — it's a line that could be a textbook example of political language games. Economy score is nearly perfect. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Yeah, why go to the doctor and get a shot for something you don't even have? / Yeah, I didn't get Clay vaccinated because it causes autism, and now he just has a little bit of autism.
jokes.setup: Jonah is pandering to anti-vaccination sentiment before a rally
jokes.punchline: Rick's casual admission that not vaccinating his son gave the child 'a little bit of autism' — presented as a reasonable outcome
jokes.explanation: 'Now he just has a little bit of autism' is the joke — the resignation in 'a little bit' and the causal logic (avoiding the vaccine caused the thing the vaccine was blamed for causing) is a darkly perfect satirical construction. Rick's obliviousness is total.

jokes.text: Yeah, and when I was a kid, they said the best case scenario was I had autism. / Fuckin' look at me now. / Don't use that line up there.
jokes.setup: The anti-vax conversation continues
jokes.punchline: Jonah reveals he was possibly autistic as a child, then uses 'fuckin' look at me now' as a triumph — Amy immediately kills it as a talking point
jokes.explanation: Jonah pointing to himself as a success story of having 'maybe autism' is a perfect piece of Jonah self-delusion. 'Fuckin' look at me now' as a triumph about his own awful personhood is both funny and a tiny bit sad. Amy's 'Don't use that line' is the right button.

jokes.text: Rick, there's a gun in the front row. / What? Jonah? / Ha ha ha ha! Made you look!
jokes.setup: Jonah is about to go onstage with his Secret Service detail
jokes.punchline: Jonah fakes out his Secret Service detail with 'there's a gun in the front row' — a 'made you look' prank — immediately before going onstage
jokes.explanation: Jonah pranking his Secret Service protection with a 'made you look' right before going onstage is exactly his character — childish, dangerous, oblivious. Good comic beat.

jokes.text: Wish me luck, meat shield.
jokes.setup: Jonah is about to go onstage
jokes.punchline: He addresses his Secret Service agent as 'meat shield' — a video game term for human protection used as a disposable barrier
jokes.explanation: 'Meat shield' is an excellent coinage for a Secret Service agent — comes from gaming culture, perfectly captures the transactional/callous view of human protection. Very economical and quotable.

jokes.text: Right. If I could digress from my prepared remarks... / How would anyone know?
jokes.setup: Jonah announces he wants to digress from his prepared remarks onstage
jokes.punchline: Amy's aside: 'How would anyone know?' — implying his remarks are so incoherent that a digression would be indistinguishable from the speech
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words ('How would anyone know?') deliver a complete character assassination of Jonah as a public speaker. Clean structure.

jokes.text: When are you from? When! Are! You! From?! / (crowd chanting) When are you from?
jokes.setup: Jonah starts attacking Selina for lying about her age, asking 'How old are you really?'
jokes.punchline: The age attack mutates into 'When are you from?' — a nonsensical reframing of age as a temporal origin question — and a rally crowd adopts it as a chant
jokes.explanation: Brilliant political satire. 'When are you from?' is a perfect parody of Trumpian rally rhetoric — semantically meaningless, grammatically strange, but rhythmically chantable. The crowd adopting it is the perfect image of how political movements absorb nonsense. The escalation to full chant is Veep at its most biting. Very quotable.

jokes.text: They have an actual crowd there? / What, are they giving away free Tommy Bahama dick cozies?
jokes.setup: Selina learns Jonah has an actual crowd at his rally
jokes.punchline: Her explanation: they must be giving away 'Tommy Bahama dick cozies' — a Florida-specific, hilariously specific brand/product combination
jokes.explanation: Exceptional specificity. Tommy Bahama (Florida-coded resort wear brand) + 'dick cozies' (a Florida Man-level item) is a perfect combination. The specificity is what elevates it above a generic insult — it's geographic, cultural, and vulgar all at once. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Just show 'em your birth certificate. / While we're at it, why don't we show 'em my estrogen patch prescription and a Polaroid of my stretch marks? / You mean your wisdom stripes.
jokes.setup: Selina is pressured to respond to Jonah's age attacks by releasing her birth certificate
jokes.punchline: She escalates to estrogen patches and stretch mark Polaroids; Gary rebrands stretch marks as 'wisdom stripes'
jokes.explanation: 'Wisdom stripes' from Gary is a lovely piece of Gary-brand unconditional devotion — he reframes Selina's insecurity as a positive. The escalation from birth certificate to estrogen patch to stretch mark Polaroid is well-constructed. Good character integration for both.

jokes.text: I have always been open and transparent about how old I am. / Yeah, 55. / I believe it's 53. / Late 40s. / You're all correct.
jokes.setup: Selina claims transparency about her age
jokes.punchline: Each staffer gives a different (lower) number; Selina tells them 'You're all correct' — confirming she's been lying to each individually
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure. The progression (55→53→Late 40s) works as an escalating joke, with 'You're all correct' as a perfect Selina payoff — she's maintained different age lies with different people. The structural efficiency is high. Great character moment.

jokes.text: Gary, I need to switch out my estrogen patch. / Mm. Should I slip in a little swirl of progesterone for you? / You can surprise me.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Gary to change her estrogen patch
jokes.punchline: Gary's medical-personal service is so intimate he's offering hormone customization; 'You can surprise me' treats her HRT like a barista order
jokes.explanation: The Gary-Selina servitude dynamic is perfectly captured — he's become her hormone manager. 'You can surprise me' is an oddly intimate/casual delivery that characterizes their weird codependency. Good character work.

jokes.text: Me so complicit. Me go jail long time.
jokes.setup: After the hormone patch exchange in front of her team, Selina acknowledges they know everything about her crimes
jokes.punchline: She parodies 'Me love you long time' to confess her complicity — a flippant, broken-English self-indictment
jokes.explanation: The 'me so complicit' construction is somewhat clever as a self-parody, but the 'me love you long time' reference is an outdated, racially coded joke that feels like a lazy reach. Slightly undercuts the joke's overall quality.

jokes.text: All right. The Houston Chronicle is reporting that Kemi voters have somehow got the idea that if they vote in the primaries, they're gonna get an IRS audit.
jokes.setup: Election update — a disinformation campaign is suppressing Kemi's Black voters
jokes.punchline: The casual framing ('have somehow got the idea') while knowing exactly what happened makes it darkly funny voter suppression satire
jokes.explanation: Strong callback to the opening voter suppression joke. The episode is building a consistent dark satire about election manipulation. 'Have somehow got the idea' is perfect passive voice comedy.

jokes.text: Keith, I don't want to know how you did that. / Well, Facebook'll walk you through it step by step. / You know what? Maybe it's best if you don't update us so much.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Keith she doesn't want to know how the disinformation was spread
jokes.punchline: Keith reveals it was just Facebook — publicly available tools — and Selina immediately regrets knowing
jokes.explanation: Sharp platform satire — the joke that election manipulation is just step-by-step on Facebook is both absurdist and eerily credible in 2019. The 'maybe it's best if you don't update us so much' reaction is a great plausible deniability beat.

jokes.text: Is Andrew tied in with the Chinese? / I-I don't know. I mean, he's always had a thing for Asians. / So do I, but if Andrew starts talking to the grand jury about, you know, you and China--
jokes.setup: Ben asks if Andrew has Chinese connections
jokes.punchline: Selina's defense of Andrew having 'a thing for Asians' treated as personal preference rather than business dealings — followed by Ben's 'So do I' as a casual aside
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the casual ambiguity of 'thing for Asians' and Ben's matching admission, but it runs a bit close to the racial stereotype gag register without quite earning it. Functional scene-building comedy.

jokes.text: This was supposed to be just a little simple bit of election-rigging! / Fuck!
jokes.setup: Selina is overwhelmed by the cascading consequences of her schemes
jokes.punchline: 'Just a little simple bit of election-rigging' — the absurd minimization of election fraud as a minor administrative task gone sideways
jokes.explanation: Outstanding line. The 'just a little simple bit of' construction applied to election-rigging is perfectly Selina — her solipsism reduces a constitutional crime to an inconvenient errand gone wrong. The 'Fuck!' tag is the right button. Highly quotable and deeply character-specific. One of the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: I have your estrogen patch, if you'd like. / You wear it. Maybe you'll grow some hair on your vagina!
jokes.setup: Gary helpfully offers the estrogen patch at the worst possible moment
jokes.punchline: Selina's rage redirects to Gary's genitalia and hormone levels
jokes.explanation: Good callback — the estrogen patch becomes a weapon of abuse. 'Maybe you'll grow some hair on your vagina' is a vivid, specific insult. Gary's timing is perfect: offering a helpful thing at the absolute wrong moment is a recurring Gary beat.

jokes.text: Do you know anything about pesticides? / Not as much as I'd like.
jokes.setup: Sidney asks Richard about his pesticide knowledge as a prelude to a corrupt offer
jokes.punchline: 'Not as much as I'd like' — Richard's earnest, curious response to a corrupt politician's question treats pesticides as a genuine knowledge gap he'd like to fill
jokes.explanation: Perfect Richard line. The joke is entirely in his delivery and intent — he genuinely wants to learn about pesticides. His response to a corrupt setup is to wish he knew more about the subject matter. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: You know, down at the feed store they're saying your new seeds cause cancer. / Well, we've paid for seven studies that say that it doesn't. / Seven? Wow. / Yeah, seven. Lucky sevens. / That's good enough for me. Can't argue with science.
jokes.setup: Richard mentions he's heard the seeds cause cancer
jokes.punchline: Sidney's defense (we paid for seven studies) and Richard's genuine acceptance ('Can't argue with science') satirizes industry-funded research and naive civic trust simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of industry-funded science and political gullibility. 'Lucky sevens' from Sidney is a great detail — he's openly admitting the studies are arbitrary. Richard's 'Can't argue with science' while not understanding the science is a perfectly character-integrated joke.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, um, you look so familiar to me. / Mmm. / Did, uh, did we...? / Oh, no! I'm an OB-GYN.
jokes.setup: Dan recognizes a woman and implies they slept together
jokes.punchline: She's his OB-GYN — she knows him in a professional medical context that's even more intimate and embarrassing
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection — the expectation of a one-night stand is subverted by a medical relationship that's more intimate in a different way. The OB-GYN punchline also sets up the abortion reveal perfectly. Well-structured.

jokes.text: You brought a woman into my clinic to have her pregnancy terminated. / Could you be a little more specific? / I'm actually worried she might have some sort of thyroid issue because of her eyes. / Oh! Amy. / Yeah, Amy.
jokes.setup: The OB-GYN reveals she knows Dan from a clinic visit
jokes.punchline: Dan has brought multiple women in for abortions, then identifies 'Amy' by her thyroid-related eyes — a clinical, detached recognition process
jokes.explanation: Excellent multi-beat joke. The 'could you be more specific?' implies Dan has done this multiple times. The identification process (thyroid/eyes → Amy) is both medically absurd and character-perfect for Dan. The reveal of Amy as the patient is a fantastic pivot. Dark but earned.

jokes.text: Reminds me, I gotta Apple Pay her for my half. / Hey, can I buy ya a drink? / You can buy me half a drink.
jokes.setup: Dan recognizes he owes Amy money for the abortion
jokes.punchline: The doctor's 'you can buy me half a drink' — mirroring Dan's half-payment offer back at him — is a perfectly calibrated comeback
jokes.explanation: The 'half a drink' callback is structurally excellent — it uses Dan's transactional nature against him in a witty reversal. Economy is near-perfect. The OB-GYN gets the best of Dan in three words.

jokes.text: When women fall apart, they really fuckin' fall apart!
jokes.setup: Kemi is shown in an emotional breakdown
jokes.punchline: Selina's sexist observation — from a woman who is herself in crisis — is peak Selina hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: The irony is rich: Selina is herself falling apart while making a sexist generalization about women. It's a classic Veep character beat — Selina's total lack of self-awareness and her casual misogyny (toward other women) is signature. Highly character-integrated.

jokes.text: Did you take the coconut out of my muesli for tomorrow morning? / No. No. / And you know what you need to do? You need to use tweezers this time because I really thought I could taste your fingers.
jokes.setup: Selina is mid-crisis and pivots to demanding muesli management
jokes.punchline: Tweezers are the specified tool for coconut removal because she could previously taste Gary's fingers in her food
jokes.explanation: The Selina-Gary dynamic in miniature: a deeply controlling culinary demand mid-crisis, with the specific complaint about Gary's finger taste. 'I really thought I could taste your fingers' is wonderfully gross. Pure character comedy.

jokes.text: Wow, I've been trying to track you down for days. / Hearing you say that really takes me back.
jokes.setup: Selina tells Andrew she's been looking for him
jokes.punchline: Andrew's nostalgic response — Selina chasing him is a familiar experience from their marriage
jokes.explanation: Efficient characterization of the Meyer marriage — Andrew's casual charm in the face of Selina's urgency is a good beat. The nostalgic subtext (he used to evade her then too) adds depth.

jokes.text: About Catherine's freshman year roommate. / She had done a gap year abroad. She was not a true freshman.
jokes.setup: Selina references Andrew's past infidelity with Catherine's college roommate
jokes.punchline: Andrew's defense is that the roommate had done a gap year — therefore technically not a freshman — as if this distinction makes the betrayal acceptable
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke. Andrew's defense — 'She was not a true freshman' — is both legally precise and morally bankrupt in exactly the right Veep way. The specificity of the 'gap year abroad' technicality is what makes it great. Andrew's charm-as-deflection is perfectly captured. Very quotable.

jokes.text: I should have really married that gay guy my mother liked.
jokes.setup: Selina and Andrew's conversation is going badly
jokes.punchline: Selina's regret is not about Andrew specifically but about the road not taken — a gay man her mother approved of who would presumably have had no scandals (or different ones)
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Selina's relationship history and the hint of a lavender marriage she missed. The 'my mother liked' detail adds another layer (mother-daughter dysfunction). Economical.

jokes.text: We cleaned a teeny-tiny bit of money through the Meyer Fund -- low nine figures. / And that faith-based thing was a godsend.
jokes.setup: Andrew explains his money laundering through the Meyer Fund
jokes.punchline: 'Teeny-tiny bit of money' for 'low nine figures' (tens of millions at minimum) and 'godsend' applied to faith-based money laundering
jokes.explanation: The 'teeny-tiny' applied to nine-figure sums is a perfect Andrew construction — charming minimization of enormous crimes. 'A godsend' for the faith-based laundering is a nice inadvertent pun. Andrew's breezy criminality is well-characterized.

jokes.text: I know him as Luther. We met in Macao at the shark fights.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if Andrew knows Keith Quinn
jokes.punchline: Andrew knows Keith by an alias ('Luther') and they met at shark fights in Macao — the most Bond-villain backstory possible
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. 'We met in Macao at the shark fights' is one of the best lines in the episode — a completely throwaway detail that's so specific and insane it creates an entire world. 'Luther' as Keith's underground alias is perfect. The casualness of delivery elevates it. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Honestly, I don't think people are gonna buy that a guy who calls vaginas 'crinkum-crankums' is gonna be able to pull off some sort of multimillion-dollar fraud.
jokes.setup: Gary is being considered as a potential fall guy for the money laundering
jokes.punchline: Andrew dismisses Gary's viability as a fraudster because he calls vaginas 'crinkum-crankums' — an absurdist character detail used as a legal defense
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode, possibly the series. 'Crinkum-crankums' as Gary's euphemism for vagina is an inspired detail that encapsulates his entire character in one word: childish, prudish, devoted, harmless. The logic that someone who uses such language couldn't commit multimillion-dollar fraud is simultaneously absurd and oddly compelling. The originality of 'crinkum-crankums' alone is worth a 9. Extremely quotable.

jokes.text: Besides: women's prison. / Yeah. / Wall-to-wall crinkum-crankum.
jokes.setup: Marjorie is proposed as a fall guy/gal instead of Gary
jokes.punchline: Andrew's endorsement of framing Marjorie: women's prison = 'wall-to-wall crinkum-crankum' — using Gary's euphemism for the most depraved purpose
jokes.explanation: Immediate callback that uses 'crinkum-crankum' in an even more transgressive context — Andrew endorsing framing his ex's daughter's partner for prison because he finds the lesbian prison scenario appealing. The reuse of the coinage is structurally excellent. Deeply dark, deeply character-specific for Andrew.

jokes.text: God, is that what a real orgasm feels like? / Ugh. Do women have those?
jokes.setup: Amy has just vented about Selina with genuine pleasure/catharsis
jokes.punchline: Amy compares her cathartic rage to an orgasm; Jonah's 'Do women have those?' is both a dig at Amy and a question revealing his ignorance about women
jokes.explanation: Good character beat. Jonah's obliviousness about female pleasure is consistent characterization. Amy's orgasm simile is a good beat of her genuine hatred for Selina. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Oh, and I should probably call Rick's mom too, just because she's probably still pretty sore from last night when I was all up in that... ♪ Puss-ay-ay-yay ♪
jokes.setup: Jonah mentions calling his mom
jokes.punchline: He reveals he slept with his staffer Rick's mother last night — sung to emphasize the vulgarity
jokes.explanation: Classic Jonah — unexpected sexual revelation delivered with complete lack of social awareness. The sung portion is a nice cringe escalation. Not the sharpest Jonah moment but consistent.

jokes.text: I think you know I'm a very traditional woman, though I do seek to topple the patriarchy by queering hetero-normative tropes. / Are you talking about your outfit?
jokes.setup: Marjorie begins a serious speech about her identity
jokes.punchline: Selina's interruption — 'Are you talking about your outfit?' — reduces Marjorie's ideological statement to a fashion comment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Selina interruption. The joke is in the compression — Marjorie's earnest ideological statement collapsed into a fashion query. Works as both a character joke for Selina (impatient, shallow) and Marjorie (her appearance does read as queer/nonconformist). Excellent economy.

jokes.text: I love the work that you do on the Meyer Fund very much. / Thank you, ma'am. / Although I personally don't know anything about it / because you're the complete mastermind behind it, right?
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to get Marjorie to confirm she ran the Meyer Fund — while being recorded
jokes.punchline: Selina's increasingly obvious attempts to make Marjorie confess on tape, with the dramatic pauses making the manipulation transparent
jokes.explanation: Good cringe scene — Selina's recording attempt is obvious from the ellipses and leading questions. The comedy is in watching Selina being transparently manipulative and Marjorie catching on. Well-structured sustained discomfort.

jokes.text: Are you recording this conversation, ma'am? / That is a bizarre thing to say. / Well, the flowers have been moved, and... Andrew's phone.
jokes.setup: Marjorie suspects she's being recorded and asks
jokes.punchline: Selina calls it 'bizarre' — the most brazen denial — while Marjorie immediately identifies the physical evidence (moved flowers, Andrew's planted phone)
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's investigative competence contrasted with Selina's crude manipulation is the comedy. The 'flowers have been moved' is a nice Marjorie character detail — her ex-Secret Service training surfaces. Selina calling it 'bizarre' is perfectly shameless.

jokes.text: They're not misdeeds. / They were exploited opportunities.
jokes.setup: Marjorie accuses Selina of financial misdeeds
jokes.punchline: Selina's semantic correction: 'exploited opportunities' rather than 'misdeeds'
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy — one line reframes corruption as entrepreneurship. 'Exploited opportunities' is a genuine corporate/political euphemism elevated to the level of actual wit. Very quotable, perfect Selina self-justification. Nearly nine words that capture her entire worldview.

jokes.text: ...by some magical combination of abuse and neglect managed to create the perfect daughter.
jokes.setup: Marjorie is saying something seemingly complimentary about Selina and Andrew as parents
jokes.punchline: The method of creating Catherine is 'abuse and neglect' — delivered as a factual observation before the compliment lands
jokes.explanation: Marjorie's forensic, emotionless delivery of 'abuse and neglect' as a parenting methodology is perfect — she states it as a fact, not a criticism, which makes it darker and funnier. The compliment ('perfect daughter') lands surprisingly after it.

jokes.text: I would like to ask both of you for Catherine's hand in marriage. / Oh! / Whoa! / Wow. / You have our blessing. / Yes, you have our blessing. Absolutely.
jokes.setup: The whole conversation seemed to be about the Meyer Fund and legal jeopardy
jokes.punchline: Marjorie pivots to a marriage proposal for Catherine — completely blindsiding Selina and Andrew, who give immediate enthusiastic approval despite everything
jokes.explanation: Great scene reversal — the tense legal-maneuvering scene ends with a marriage proposal that Selina and Andrew enthusiastically bless despite being in the middle of trying to frame Marjorie. Their relief ('Yes, absolutely') is the joke. The pivot is structurally clean.

jokes.text: Can I ask you a question, Marjorie? / Of course, ma'am. / Why would you buy that cow? / Oh. When I can get the milk for free. Very good, ma'am.
jokes.setup: After giving the marriage blessing, Selina asks why Marjorie would propose
jokes.punchline: Marjorie understands immediately and completes the idiom — applying 'why buy the cow' to herself and Catherine — then rates Selina's joke
jokes.explanation: Marjorie completing and rating the joke ('Very good, ma'am') is the best part — she's so precise and deadpan that even receiving a backhanded cow metaphor is handled with clinical professionalism. Selina's casual use of it about her own daughter is dark.

jokes.text: I still have Mee-Maw's boat. / The Labor Day? / Mm-hmm. / You told me it sank. / I told you I reported it sunk to insurance.
jokes.setup: Andrew announces an escape plan involving a boat
jokes.punchline: The boat was reported sunk for insurance fraud — Andrew's backstory has more crime embedded in every detail
jokes.explanation: The distinction between 'it sank' and 'I reported it sunk to insurance' is a perfect Andrew line — casual insurance fraud as background detail of a romantic escape plan. Good economy.

jokes.text: You know I've long felt a deep connection with the land of Israel and their non-extradition treaty.
jokes.setup: Andrew is planning his escape to Cuba, then suggests his final destination
jokes.punchline: His 'deep connection' with Israel is explicitly about the non-extradition treaty — the sentimentality is entirely transactional
jokes.explanation: Excellent. The joke combines Andrew's WASP entitlement (casually claiming a 'deep connection' with Israel) with pure pragmatism (non-extradition treaty). The construction — 'I have long felt / and their non-extradition treaty' — perfectly sets up and punctures the sentimentality. Very quotable.

jokes.text: You can't just disappear, and I can't have people thinking I'm Jewish. / We're right in the middle of the primaries.
jokes.setup: Andrew suggests they go to Israel together
jokes.punchline: Selina's first objection to fleeing to Israel is that people might think she's Jewish — a mid-primary political concern about religious perception
jokes.explanation: The priority (don't appear Jewish during primaries) before the obvious problem (fleeing justice) is darkly funny. 'We're right in the middle of the primaries' as a reason not to be perceived as Jewish is bleak and satirically pointed.

jokes.text: The only thing they have on you is me. / Well, that's the most romantic thing you've ever said to me.
jokes.setup: Andrew explains why he's leaving — he's the only evidence against her
jokes.punchline: Selina treats his decision to become a fugitive to protect her as a romantic gesture
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character beat — the Meyer marriage rendered in miniature: romance as legal strategy. 'Most romantic thing you've ever said to me' applied to evidence-suppression is perfectly Selina and Andrew. Good economy.

jokes.text: But I'm gonna need some walkin'-around money. / Here we go. What-- I mean, how far do you have to walk?
jokes.setup: Andrew has agreed to flee the country to protect Selina
jokes.punchline: He immediately asks for money; Selina's 'here we go' and 'how far do you have to walk' — catching the euphemism before pretending not to understand it
jokes.explanation: Good beat of Selina's exasperation with Andrew — 'here we go' as a reaction beat, then the literal interpretation ('how far do you have to walk?') is a nice piece of wordplay. Character-consistent for both.

jokes.text: Your false accusations are working, ma'am. / Oh. Then they're no longer false.
jokes.setup: Keith reports that the planted stories about Kemi's husband are gaining traction
jokes.punchline: Selina's instant logical reframe — 'then they're no longer false' — is a perfect political epistemology joke: truth is what succeeds
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. Six words encapsulate an entire political philosophy about truth, media, and power. The structure is perfect (logical inversion), the economy is exceptional (9.5), and the character integration is pitch-perfect Selina. Worthy of 8+ with clear justification.

jokes.text: That's the best explanation of democracy I've ever heard.
jokes.setup: Someone describes the primary as 'basically a two-woman race to see who is less offensive to the American people'
jokes.punchline: Kent treats this cynical description as genuinely the best democratic explanation he's encountered
jokes.explanation: Clean Kent deadpan. His clinical acceptance of this as the ideal democratic description is character-perfect. Good economy.

jokes.text: Also, they found Rosa Sanchez in El Salvador. / Who? / The fake real woman from your speech? / Just in the nick of time. She was about to be stoned by the local child army.
jokes.setup: Update on the deported Rosa Sanchez
jokes.punchline: Rosa was found in El Salvador about to be stoned by a 'local child army' — the situation keeps getting worse for this innocent woman Selina conjured
jokes.explanation: Strong callback and escalation — the Rosa saga keeps worsening. 'Fake real woman' is a nice compression of the joke's premise. 'Local child army' is a darkly absurdist detail that perfectly captures Central American political violence as background noise for Selina's campaign. Selina's 'Who?' also perfectly captures her total indifference to the person she created.

jokes.text: I don't like when people feel they have to get closer to talk to me. / It usually means I'm gonna be facing a longer prison sentence.
jokes.setup: Her team approaches with grim news after Andrew is found dead
jokes.punchline: Selina has learned that physical proximity from advisors correlates with bad legal news
jokes.explanation: Good Selina beat — the experienced politician has learned to read her team's body language as a legal threat indicator. The 'longer prison sentence' specificity is the right punchline.

jokes.text: We talked about a trip he might take out of the country... / I will add tampering to our list of charges. / But if we were able to give him a going-away gift, uh, he might be able to turn it into a stay-cation. / And bribery.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to explain her conversation with Andrew without admitting obstruction
jokes.punchline: Kent adds legal charges in real time as Selina talks; the euphemism 'going-away gift' / 'stay-cation' while Kent tallies offenses
jokes.explanation: Kent as real-time legal charge accumulator is an excellent structural joke. 'And bribery' as the button after the staycation euphemism is perfectly timed. Selina's attempt to soften obstruction with 'going-away gift/stay-cation' wordplay is funny.

jokes.text: How much is left in that whole faith-space-- / Kent: Faith-based. / I spent it all on Bibles for the homeless.
jokes.setup: Selina needs the remaining faith-based money for a bribe
jokes.punchline: Gary spent ALL of it on Bibles for the homeless
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gary callback. The faith-based money was supposed to be spent on absurd religious projects; Gary went fully earnest and spent it on homeless Bibles. His total sincerity in a corrupt context is exquisite character comedy. 'I spent it all on Bibles for the homeless' delivered with Gary's earnestness against Selina's crisis is one of the episode's best Gary moments.

jokes.text: You spent millions... / Yes, yes, I did. / ...on leather-bound hobo toilet paper. / Mmm. I hope they don't use the New Testament for that.
jokes.setup: Selina reacts to Gary spending millions on Bibles for the homeless
jokes.punchline: 'Leather-bound hobo toilet paper' as Selina's reframe; Ben's addition — hoping they don't use the New Testament specifically
jokes.explanation: 'Leather-bound hobo toilet paper' is an excellent coinage. Ben's follow-up about the New Testament is the perfect dark religious button. The two-line escalation is well-constructed. 'Leather-bound hobo toilet paper' is highly quotable.

jokes.text: I was an alcoholic, I was in a relationship with a blonde, uncaring bug-eyed rage-aholic. / I used to work with someone just like that.
jokes.setup: A radio caller describes their past troubled relationship
jokes.punchline: Mike's response — 'I used to work with someone just like that' — is a perfectly inadvertent self-burn: the 'bug-eyed rage-aholic' is Selina Meyer, his former boss
jokes.explanation: Good self-referential joke — Mike doesn't realize he's describing Selina, which makes it a classic straight-man/unknowing participant joke. 'Bug-eyed rage-aholic' is a great descriptor. The audience knows Mike worked for Selina, so the connection lands well.

jokes.text: What's going on with this chair? It's too low. / Hint: We could still use the changing table, or bye-bye ping pong. / Oh, that's actually a cute name. Are you gonna keep that?
jokes.setup: Selina calls Mike's show and he makes an aside about a baby shower gift
jokes.punchline: Selina treats 'bye-bye ping pong' as a potential baby name, completely missing (or ignoring) Mike's context
jokes.explanation: Selina's narcissistic misreading of 'bye-bye ping pong' as a baby name is a good character beat. Slightly underpowered but functional.

jokes.text: I need some help for my ex from your friends in the East. / Oh, I thought we were putting our little arrangement on the back burner?
jokes.setup: Selina uses Mike's call-in show as a channel to communicate with Keith/Chinese handlers
jokes.punchline: Selina is requesting help disposing of her ex-husband through a live radio show
jokes.explanation: The comedy is structural — using a live radio program as a back channel for criminal conspiracy. The absurdity of this communication method is the joke. Effective cringe moment on rewatch knowing what it leads to.

jokes.text: My daughter Catherine is getting married to her lady friend Marjorie. / That's amazing! Isn't that great, buddy? / Well, the Bible is actually very clear-- / Selina: But now I have to run.
jokes.setup: Selina announces Catherine's engagement to change the subject from political controversy
jokes.punchline: The co-host starts to object citing the Bible; Selina immediately hangs up
jokes.explanation: Selina using Catherine's engagement as a distraction device — and immediately hanging up when it goes wrong — is good character comedy. The speed of the hang-up is the punchline.

jokes.text: Any date for the wedding? / Huh? / Any date for the wedding? / Oh, who gives a shit.
jokes.setup: After announcing Catherine's engagement as a PR maneuver
jokes.punchline: 'Who gives a shit' — Selina has zero interest in her daughter's actual wedding
jokes.explanation: Clean character beat. The contrast between Selina weaponizing the engagement and then having zero investment in the actual event is sharp. Good economy.

jokes.text: Are you referring to Sidney Purcell, the Con-Ag-Chem Fam Farm lobbyist? / I'm so sorry. We don't have any time for more questions. / Actually, Dan, that did sound like a question.
jokes.setup: Richard has accidentally named the lobbyist; Dan tries to shut down the presser
jokes.punchline: Richard corrects Dan — 'that did sound like a question' — with total sincerity, procedurally undermining Dan's damage control
jokes.explanation: Perfect Richard moment — his procedural fairness overrides Dan's spin attempt. 'That did sound like a question' is delivered with complete earnestness, not as a challenge to Dan but as a genuine correction of process. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: No, that's not what he said. He didn't say that. / No, no, no, Dan is right. / Dan: Thank you. / Sidney made the offer. Con-Ag-Chem is a company, it can't talk. / Hilarious. A talking company. Where would the mouth even be?
jokes.setup: Dan tries to walk back Richard's admission; Richard tries to help while making it worse
jokes.punchline: Richard's correction — 'Sidney made the offer, not the company, companies can't talk' — is technically accurate and legally worse; his follow-up musing about where a company's mouth would be
jokes.explanation: Outstanding Richard moment. The logic is perfect: Richard's correction (companies can't make offers, people do) is simultaneously legally accurate and politically disastrous. His genuine philosophical follow-up ('Where would the mouth even be?') is the cap. It's Richard's entire character: good-faith literalism that perfectly destroys Dan's spin. The 'Hilarious' from Dan — acknowledging Richard's obliviousness — makes it land. One of the episode's best jokes.

jokes.text: Your mother's boat, the Labor Day, exploded off the coast of Florida this afternoon. / Andrew was the only person aboard. / No, uh-uh. 'Cause he was going to Cuba. / A... lledgedly.
jokes.setup: Kent delivers news of Andrew's death in a boat explosion
jokes.punchline: Selina's denial ('No, uh-uh, 'cause he was going to Cuba') followed by Kent's 'A...llegedly' — the most drawn-out legal qualifier possible
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. Selina's shocked denial using Andrew's planned escape route as evidence he couldn't be dead is darkly funny. Kent's 'A...llegedly' — drawn out for comedic effect, covering both Cuba and the murder — is a perfectly calibrated Kent beat. The pause between 'A' and 'llegedly' is one of the best single moments in the episode. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, wow. Rosa. / Oh, my good friend. / That's not Rosa. / N-- Oh? Oh! / There she is. / That must be your mother. / My sister. / Your si-- sister. OK.
jokes.setup: Selina meets the real Rosa Sanchez for a photo op
jokes.punchline: She grabs the wrong woman — then the wrong second woman — before finding Rosa
jokes.explanation: Physical/cringe comedy — Selina's inability to identify the woman she allegedly knows personally reveals she's never actually met her (invented her). The escalation through wrong women works as farce. Good character beat.

jokes.text: They put me on a plane and sent me to Mexico. / That's a terrible... place. / I did nothing wrong. / No, well, I did nothing wrong. / I'm-- I'm just trying to run for president here. / You don't know how lucky you have it, really, to tell you the truth.
jokes.setup: Rosa confronts Selina about being deported because of her speech
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to a deportee telling her she was deported: 'You don't know how lucky you have it'
jokes.explanation: Devastating character comedy. Selina tells a woman who was just deported because of Selina's speech that she 'doesn't know how lucky she has it' — comparing Rosa's deportation favorably to Selina's political problems. Selina's oblivious self-pity reaching this level is both the comedy and the horror of the character. Excellent.

jokes.text: I want to be alone. (to camera)
jokes.setup: Rosa thanks Selina as 'President Montez' (confusing her with the actual president); Selina's moment of reflection after Andrew's death and the Rosa disaster
jokes.punchline: Selina's Garbo-esque 'I want to be alone' — delivered to the documentary camera — is both pathos and comedy
jokes.explanation: Brief but effective beat. The pathos of the moment mixed with the Garbo reference — and the fact that she's saying it to a camera crew — adds a layer of dark absurdism. Mostly a tonal beat rather than a pure joke.

jokes.text: Mom, I can't believe that you ruined Marjorie's proposal all over the internet. / Catherine, I have just gotten some terrible news about your father, uh, being lost at sea, and you are up my ass about some proposal bullshit? For real?
jokes.setup: Catherine calls to complain about the proposal being posted online
jokes.punchline: Selina announces Andrew's death as 'lost at sea' buried in a complaint about Catherine's priorities
jokes.explanation: Selina deploying Andrew's death as a conversation-ender in a mother-daughter argument is characteristic. 'Being lost at sea' as the euphemism (he was blown up) while making it about her own complaint is dark and well-delivered.

jokes.text: Andrew is blown up. / Did you do that? Did you fucking do that?! / Hey, hey, hey, hey! Hey! / I have no idea what you're talking about, Madame President, but you did say to take care of it.
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Keith about Andrew's death
jokes.punchline: Keith denies knowledge while simultaneously confirming his interpretation of 'take care of it'
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff. 'I have no idea what you're talking about, but you did say to take care of it' is a perfect plausible deniability joke — he's simultaneously denying and confirming. The admission buried in the denial is well-constructed.

jokes.text: OK, sure, but I didn't-- I didn't say it in a 'take care of it' kind of way. / I just said take care of it. / And it was pretty clear. You said, 'Take care of it,' / and then you gave me a look. / Not a 'blow him to kingdom-fucking-come' look! / No, no, no. Listen, your eyebrows did not move at all. / Because I have had Botox!
jokes.setup: Selina insists she didn't mean for Keith to have Andrew killed
jokes.punchline: Keith argues her facial expression was ambiguous; Selina's defense — she couldn't convey intent because of Botox
jokes.explanation: THE standout joke of the episode and potentially of the series. The escalation is flawless: 'take care of it' → ambiguous look → 'not a blow him to kingdom-fucking-come look' → 'your eyebrows didn't move' → 'BECAUSE I HAVE HAD BOTOX.' Every step is perfect. The Botox defense for an accidental murder order is completely original, structurally immaculate, character-perfect (Selina's vanity literally caused a man's death through communication failure), and absolutely devastating in impact. This is a 9+ joke. The closest thing in this episode to the 'Snip snap' calibration example.

jokes.text: We have to find someone who can read Mandarin but have no idea what it means. / Like a brain-damaged Chinaman. / Or woman. / Or woman. Sorry. / Or the adopted child of someone with brain damage.
jokes.setup: They need someone to read a Mandarin document without understanding the implications
jokes.punchline: The escalating specifications for their ideal translator: brain-damaged, Chinese, corrected for gender, then the adopted child alternative
jokes.explanation: The 'brain-damaged Chinaman' joke leans too hard on slur-as-punchline without enough wit to justify it. The 'or woman / or woman, sorry' gender correction is a decent riff on casual PC self-correction, but the overall joke is weaker than Veep's best — it's reaching for transgressive without sufficient craft. Below average for this episode.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. No, that makes sense, I was born in Toronto. / Toronto... Canada?
jokes.setup: Jonah's birth certificate can't be found in New Hampshire records
jokes.punchline: He was born in Canada — which he's treating as a minor detail; Amy's 'Toronto... Canada?' is the shocked reality check
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke — it mirrors and inverts the birther movement plot against Selina, but Jonah is actually Canadian. His casual 'that makes sense' as if this is a minor administrative detail, and Amy's delayed 'Toronto... Canada?' are perfectly timed. The irony of the man attacking Selina's birth certificate being born in Canada is structurally delicious.

jokes.text: Uh-oh, I think I know what that's about. / (Agent Youngblood smiles knowingly at Jonah's mother's nausea)
jokes.setup: Jonah's mother is suddenly nauseous
jokes.punchline: Jonah implies Youngblood has been with his mother; the agent's reaction suggests it
jokes.explanation: Setup for the Youngblood-mother revelation. The reaction beat works — the implication is clear. Not the strongest joke but plants the seed effectively.

jokes.text: I have repeatedly called for Selina Meyer to release her birth certificate, but as everybody on my elementary school playground knows, I am not afraid to show you mine.
jokes.setup: Jonah's press conference to release his birth certificate
jokes.punchline: 'As everybody on my elementary school playground knows' — a bizarre credential for not being afraid to show things; also: Jonah's biography is always weirdly specific
jokes.explanation: The elementary school playground credential is a good Jonah specificity. The mirroring of the birther attack is still the main comedy motor here. Functional.

jokes.text: Name: Jonah Joy Ryan. / Weight: six pounds. Length: twenty-six inches. / Yeah, that's right, twenty-six inches.
jokes.setup: Jonah reads his birth certificate aloud
jokes.punchline: The length at birth is 26 inches — implausibly large — and he repeats it with pride, implying he sees birth length as a size boast
jokes.explanation: Jonah reading birth stats as a flex is perfect character comedy. 26 inches at birth is in the plausible range but he treats it as a virility boast. 'Yeah, that's right, twenty-six inches' said to a crowd is both absurd and deeply Jonah.

jokes.text: Father... [pause] / Excuse me. / Beth: Jonah. / [Jonah looks at the birth certificate in shock]
jokes.setup: Jonah is reading his birth certificate at a press conference and reaches the 'Father' line
jokes.punchline: The pause and 'Excuse me' at a press conference as Jonah discovers live that his father is not who he thought
jokes.explanation: Masterful comedic timing beat. The joke is the pause itself — Jonah, at a press conference, discovering a family secret live on camera. The structure (building to 'Father...' then silence) is impeccable. 'Excuse me.' said to a press corps is a beautifully economical punchline. The silence is the joke. Perfect escalation of the birth certificate subplot.

jokes.text: Lloyd is my real dad? / Oh, of course he is. / I tried to tell you a hundred times, Jonie. / Do you want a snack? / No. I'm not talking to y-- / Fine. Mac and cheese. / I can't believe this. / And ketchup.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just discovered his stepfather is his biological father
jokes.punchline: His mother's response to the crisis is to offer snacks; Jonah's principled refusal to talk to her, immediately abandoned for mac and cheese ('and ketchup')
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation. The snack offer during an existential family crisis is perfectly maternal and deflecting. Jonah's 'No. I'm not talking to y--' immediately caving to 'Fine. Mac and cheese' regresses him to childhood instantly. 'And ketchup' is the perfect cap — he's negotiating condiments while discovering his parentage. Great structure.

jokes.text: That's why we went to Canada. We didn't tell anyone because Lloyd and I are second cousins. / God, Mom, that is vomitrocious.
jokes.setup: The mother explains why she gave birth in Canada — to hide the identity of the father
jokes.punchline: Lloyd is both Jonah's stepfather AND second cousin; 'vomitrocious' as Jonah's invented word
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the Jonah family horror. The second-cousin stepfather reveal is perfectly timed. 'Vomitrocious' is a great Jonah coinage — he invents words under stress. The absurdity escalates: he's Canadian, his father is his stepfather, who is his second cousin, and he was conceived at what was presumably a Canadian family gathering.

jokes.text: I could have been born with something wrong with me. / ...I never thought that this would be a problem...
jokes.setup: Jonah is horrified about the consanguinity of his parents
jokes.punchline: The joke is that Jonah IS something wrong — the irony of him worrying about birth defects when he is the most monstrous person in the room
jokes.explanation: The implicit irony — Jonah's horror at possibly being born with 'something wrong' when he is demonstrably the wrongest person alive — is the joke. His mother's breezy 'I never thought this would be a problem' is both terrible parenting and perfect comedy.

jokes.text: Wait, is Lloyd still my dad? / Yeah, nobody knows. / But you're now officially my stepsister. / Half sister.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to process the Lloyd relationship
jokes.punchline: Nobody knows if Lloyd is the biological father — but Beth (his girlfriend) is now his half-sister regardless
jokes.explanation: Another escalation beat in the Jonah family saga. 'Nobody knows' is a great maternal shrug about paternity. The reveal that Beth — Jonah's girlfriend — is at minimum his step/half-sister is the episode's most sustained cringe escalation. 'Half sister' from Beth as a correction while she's dating him is the right precision beat.

jokes.text: I want Lloyd to not be my dad! / Me too.
jokes.setup: Jonah wants his paternity reversed
jokes.punchline: His mother agrees she also doesn't want Lloyd to be his dad — said with complete sincerity and resignation
jokes.explanation: Perfect button for the Jonah family scene. 'Me too' from his mother — two words expressing shared regret about Jonah's existence and paternity — is devastatingly economical. It's both a joke about her regret and a perfect maternal resignation beat. Great economy score.

jokes.text: Yes, but often, when a child gets a new baby brother, they can feel neglected and-- and low self-esteem. / Catherine, for one. / Well, yes, exactly. / And she didn't even have a baby brother. So--
jokes.setup: Selina and Mike discuss a baby's impact on older siblings
jokes.punchline: Selina uses Catherine as the example of low self-esteem — then the follow-up: Catherine didn't even have a baby brother, so Selina caused that damage with no trigger
jokes.explanation: Excellent parenting comedy. The escalation — Catherine had self-esteem issues, then the reveal there was no baby brother to trigger them (Selina just did that naturally) — is structurally perfect. Selina's casual delivery of her own maternal failures is deeply character-integrated.

jokes.text: I just talked to my guy in the Southern District. / They're dropping the investigation into the Meyer Fund. / No! / Without Andrew, the whole thing just blew up. / Sorry.
jokes.setup: Selina hears the investigation is being dropped
jokes.punchline: 'Without Andrew, the whole thing just blew up.' / 'Sorry.' — Ben uses the word 'blew up' in the literal sense about the investigation, which also describes how Andrew died
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark wordplay callback. 'The whole thing just blew up' — said about a legal investigation — is also literally true about Andrew. Ben's 'Sorry' (for the poor word choice) is the perfect understated tag. The double meaning is earned and structurally tight.

jokes.text: Is this what it feels like to be a man?
jokes.setup: Good news arrives (investigation dropped, Post story spiked) after a series of crises
jokes.punchline: Selina's expression of relief is framed as a gender experience — as if political luck and relief are exclusively male
jokes.explanation: Razor-sharp feminist political satire in six words. Selina's relief at things going her way triggers 'Is this what it feels like to be a man?' — a comment on how unfamiliar political success is for her as a woman. Exceptional economy, highly quotable, and deeply character-integrated. One of the episode's best pure political jokes.

jokes.text: Who is Mon-tiz-uh? / Montez. / Wait a minute-- 'By giving Meyer the nomination, they can ensure a Montez presidency'? / I want to be president when I grow up. / You cannot.
jokes.setup: A child has been used to translate a Mandarin document revealing the Chinese preferred Selina to lose so Montez wins
jokes.punchline: The child says she wants to be president when she grows up; Selina tells her flatly 'You cannot'
jokes.explanation: Perfect button. Selina's 'You cannot' to a child's presidential aspiration is simultaneously character-specific (Selina seeing the presidency as hers), darkly funny (crushing a child's dream mid-treason revelation), and structurally clean. The contrast with the surrounding political chaos makes it hit harder.

jokes.text: We can't even commit treason right?!
jokes.setup: The Mandarin document reveals the Chinese were secretly working against Selina
jokes.punchline: Selina's outrage is not that treason occurred but that it wasn't executed competently on her behalf
jokes.explanation: An outstanding line. 'We can't even commit treason right?!' perfectly captures Selina's complete moral inversion — her complaint is quality control for high crimes. The compression is flawless. Could only be Selina Meyer. This rivals the Botox line as a best-of-episode moment. The '?!' punctuation matters — it's outrage, not resignation.

jokes.text: What about my present? / Uh, I'm giving it to your brother.
jokes.setup: Selina earlier told Ellen the present was in the car; the child asks again
jokes.punchline: Selina punishes the child's inconvenient translation by giving her present to the baby
jokes.explanation: Selina redirecting a child's present as punishment for bad news is a perfect character beat. The casualness of 'I'm giving it to your brother' as revenge on a child is darkly funny.

jokes.text: More than a dozen Iowa government officials are in hot water. The lieutenant governor, along with several state senators, have all been arrested for accepting improper campaign contributions from Con-Ag-Chem Family Farms. / Bye-bye, Sidney.
jokes.setup: News breaks about the Con-Ag-Chem arrests
jokes.punchline: Dan's satisfied 'Bye-bye, Sidney' — after Richard accidentally exposed the entire scheme — completes the arc with the smoothness of a man who knows he landed on his feet
jokes.explanation: Dan's casual satisfaction at the downfall of the corruption scheme he almost got tangled in is a nice closing beat. The callback pays off the Richard Splett storyline neatly.

jokes.text: I've got way more foreign policy experience than that half-wit Kemi. / I think it's pronounced half-white.
jokes.setup: Selina brags about foreign policy experience over Kemi
jokes.punchline: Ben corrects 'half-wit' to 'half-white' — a racial pun about Kemi's biracial identity used as a political attack
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay. The 'half-wit / half-white' pun is densely constructed: it's a phonetic near-homophone, a racial observation, and Ben's delivery as a gentle correction ('I think it's pronounced') makes it land like a precise knife. One of the cleanest bits of wordplay in the episode. Very quotable.

jokes.text: That is highly classified. / I'm sorry Selina, you have left me with no choice.
jokes.setup: Kent reveals classified financial information (wire transfer details) and Selina calls it classified
jokes.punchline: Kent responds that Selina's actions have left him with no choice — implying he's about to do something drastic
jokes.explanation: Scene-ending cliffhanger beat. The comedy is in Kent's formal declaration that he's been left with 'no choice' — Kent's precision being applied to what sounds like an ultimatum. Effective tension-with-comedy but primarily a plot beat.

jokes.text: Ha! / That was your phone.
jokes.setup: Kent delivers his 'no choice' ultimatum dramatically
jokes.punchline: Kent laughs — presumably at a phone notification — and Selina has to point out 'that was your phone,' deflating the dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect episode-ending joke. The dramatic tension of Kent's ultimatum is entirely deflated by a phone notification laugh. 'That was your phone' is an extremely economical punchline to a cliffhanger. It also perfectly characterizes Kent — unflappable, precise, now hilariously human. Great final beat.

tmdb_overview: Facing increased scrutiny heading into Super Tuesday, Selina visits Mike to deflect attention from Andrew's legal troubles. Jonah and Amy ramp up their anti-Selina campaign rhetoric. Dan attempts to exploit Richard.
insight: Political chaos peaks at 1.94 jokes per minute, yet character comedy anchors the mayhem.
